options.explanation: In a lead storage battery, all of these statements are true. Explanation: The lead storage battery is a rechargeable battery . This battery has a pure lead as anode; lead(IV) oxide as a cathode and dilute sulfuric acid as an electrolyte. When the battery is being used, lead(IV) oxide is reduced to PdSO 4 and lead is oxidized to PdSO 4 . When the battery is being charged, PdSO₄ is again converted into PbO 2 and Pb as cathode and anode respectively.

question_text: An electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell when

options.explanation: If an external opposite potential is applied on the electrochemical cell, the reaction continues to take place till the opposite voltage reaches the value 1.1 V. At this stage, no current flow through the cell and if there is any further increase in the external potential (E external ), then reaction starts functioning in opposite direction i.e. an electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell. {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {external }}>\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}{/tex}

question_text: Resistance of a conductivity cell filled with 0.1 mol L -1 KCl solution is 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . If the resistance of the same cell when filled with 0.02 mol L -1 KCl solution is 520 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , calculate the conductivity and molar conductivity of 0.02 mol L -1 KCl solution. The conductivity of 0.1 mol L -1 KCl solution is 1.29 S/m.
explanation: The cell constant is given by the equation: Cell constant = G* = conductivity {tex}\times{/tex} resistance = 1.29 S/m {tex}\times{/tex} 100 Ω = 129 m -1 = 1.29 cm -1 Conductivity of 0.02 mol L -1 KCl solution = cell constant / resistance = {tex}\frac{G^{*}}{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{129 {m}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex} = 0.248 S m -1 Concentration = 0.02 mol L -1 = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.02 mol m -3 = 20 mol m -3 Molar conductivity = A m = {tex}\frac{K}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{248 \times 10^{-3} {Sm}^{-1}}{20 {mol\;m}^{-3}}{/tex} = 124 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 S m 2 mol -1 Alternatively, conductivity of solution K = {tex}\frac{1.29 {cm}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex} = = 0.248 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 S cm -1 and molar conductivity of solution A m = K {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 cm 3 L -1 molarity -1 = {tex}\frac{0.248 \times 10^{-2} {S} {cm}^{-1} \times 1000 {cm}^{3} {L}^{-1}}{0.02 {mol} {L}^{-1}}{/tex} = 124 S cm 2 mol -1
solution: The cell constant is given by the equation: Cell constant = G* = conductivity {tex}\times{/tex} resistance = 1.29 S/m {tex}\times{/tex} 100 Ω = 129 m -1 = 1.29 cm -1 Conductivity of 0.02 mol L -1 KCl solution = cell constant / resistance = {tex}\frac{G^{*}}{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{129 {m}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex} = 0.248 S m -1 Concentration = 0.02 mol L -1 = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.02 mol m -3 = 20 mol m -3 Molar conductivity = A m = {tex}\frac{K}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{248 \times 10^{-3} {Sm}^{-1}}{20 {mol\;m}^{-3}}{/tex} = 124 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 S m 2 mol -1 Alternatively, conductivity of solution K = {tex}\frac{1.29 {cm}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex} = = 0.248 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 S cm -1 and molar conductivity of solution A m = K {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 cm 3 L -1 molarity -1 = {tex}\frac{0.248 \times 10^{-2} {S} {cm}^{-1} \times 1000 {cm}^{3} {L}^{-1}}{0.02 {mol} {L}^{-1}}{/tex} = 124 S cm 2 mol -1
solution_html:
The cell constant is given by the equation:
Cell constant = G* = conductivity {tex}\times{/tex} resistance
= 1.29 S/m {tex}\times{/tex} 100 Ω = 129 m-1 = 1.29 cm-1
Conductivity of 0.02 mol L-1 KCl solution = cell constant / resistance
= {tex}\frac{G^{*}}{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{129 {m}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex} = 0.248 S m-1
Concentration = 0.02 mol L-1 = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.02 mol m-3 = 20 mol m-3
Molar conductivity = Am = {tex}\frac{K}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{248 \times 10^{-3} {Sm}^{-1}}{20 {mol\;m}^{-3}}{/tex} = 124 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 S m2mol-1
Alternatively, conductivity of solution K = {tex}\frac{1.29 {cm}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex} = = 0.248 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 S cm-1
and molar conductivity of solution Am = K {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 cm3 L-1 molarity-1
= {tex}\frac{0.248 \times 10^{-2} {S} {cm}^{-1} \times 1000 {cm}^{3} {L}^{-1}}{0.02 {mol} {L}^{-1}}{/tex}
= 124 S cm2 mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the {tex}\Delta_rG^o{/tex} and emf (E) that can be obtained from the following cell under the standard conditions at 25oC.
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q) \| \mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q)\right| \operatorname{Sn}(s){/tex}
[Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V; {tex}E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{n}}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.14 V and F = 96500 Cmol-1].
question_text: Calculate the {tex}\Delta_rG^o{/tex} and emf (E) that can be obtained from the following cell under the standard conditions at 25 o C. {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q) \| \mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q)\right| \operatorname{Sn}(s){/tex} [Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V; {tex}E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{n}}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.14 V and F = 96500 Cmol -1 ].
explanation: At anode: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-}{/tex} At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex} Overall reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)+\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}-\left(-E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{0}\right){/tex} = -0.14 + 0.76 = 0.62 V {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}=-2 \times 96500 \times 0.62{/tex} {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-119660\; \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: At anode: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-}{/tex} At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex} Overall reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)+\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}-\left(-E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{0}\right){/tex} = -0.14 + 0.76 = 0.62 V {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}=-2 \times 96500 \times 0.62{/tex} {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-119660\; \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
At anode: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-}{/tex}
At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex}
Overall reaction:
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)+\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex}
{tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}-\left(-E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{0}\right){/tex}
= -0.14 + 0.76 = 0.62 V
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}=-2 \times 96500 \times 0.62{/tex}
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-119660\; \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} for acetic acid,
Given that,
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex}(HCI) = 426S cm2 ml-1
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex}(NaCl)=126S cm2 ml-1
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex}(CH3COONa) = 91S cm2 ml-1
question_text: Calculate {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} for acetic acid, Given that, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} (HCI) = 426S cm 2 ml -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} (NaCl)=126S cm 2 ml -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} (CH 3 COONa) = 91S cm 2 ml -1
explanation: Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}=426 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}=126 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right)}^{\circ}=91 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{cooH}\right)=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{coona}\right)-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = (426 + 91 - 126)S cm 2 mol -1 = 391S cm 2 mol -1
solution: Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}=426 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}=126 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right)}^{\circ}=91 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{cooH}\right)=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{coona}\right)-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = (426 + 91 - 126)S cm 2 mol -1 = 391S cm 2 mol -1
solution_html:
Given,
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}=426 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}=126 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right)}^{\circ}=91 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{cooH}\right)=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{coona}\right)-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
= (426 + 91 - 126)S cm2mol-1
= 391S cm2mol-1

question_text: Calculate the strength of the current required to deposit 1.2g of magnesium from molten MgCl 2 in 1h. [1F = 96500 Cmol -1 , atomic mass of Mg = 24.0].
explanation: The half reaction occurring during deposition is: Mg 2 + + 2e - ----------> Mg(s) So to produce 1 mole of Mg we need 2 moles of electrons, 1 mol of Mg = 24 g Thus 1.2 g of Mg = ( {tex}\frac{1.2}{24}{/tex} ) = 0.05 moles Hence no. of moles of electrons required to deposit = 0.05 x 2 = 0.1 moles Qty. of electricity passed (Q) = moles of electrons passed x 96500 = 0.1 x 96500 = 9650 C I = {tex}\frac Qt{/tex} I = {tex}\frac {9650}{60 \times 60}{/tex} (converting 1 hr into seconds) I = 2.68 A .
solution: The half reaction occurring during deposition is: Mg 2 + + 2e - ----------> Mg(s) So to produce 1 mole of Mg we need 2 moles of electrons, 1 mol of Mg = 24 g Thus 1.2 g of Mg = ( {tex}\frac{1.2}{24}{/tex} ) = 0.05 moles Hence no. of moles of electrons required to deposit = 0.05 x 2 = 0.1 moles Qty. of electricity passed (Q) = moles of electrons passed x 96500 = 0.1 x 96500 = 9650 C I = {tex}\frac Qt{/tex} I = {tex}\frac {9650}{60 \times 60}{/tex} (converting 1 hr into seconds) I = 2.68 A .
solution_html:
The half reaction occurring during deposition is:
Mg2+ + 2e- ----------> Mg(s)
So to produce 1 mole of Mg we need 2 moles of electrons,
1 mol of Mg = 24 g
Thus 1.2 g of Mg = ({tex}\frac{1.2}{24}{/tex}) = 0.05 moles
Hence no. of moles of electrons required to deposit  = 0.05 x 2 = 0.1 moles
Qty. of electricity passed (Q) = moles of electrons passed x 96500
 = 0.1 x 96500 = 9650 C
I = {tex}\frac Qt{/tex}
I = {tex}\frac {9650}{60 \times 60}{/tex} (converting 1 hr into seconds)
I = 2.68 A.

question_text: Eº (Cu 2+ /Cu) and Eº (Ag + /Ag) is + 0.337 V and + 0.799 V respectively. Make a cell whose EMF is +ve. If the concentration of Cu 2+ is 0.01M and E cell at 25ºC is zero, calculate the concentration of Ag + .
explanation: Cu is more reactive than silver, so that the cell is as Cu/Cu 2+ (0.01M) || Ag + (C)/Ag or cell reaction {tex}\mathrm{Cu}+2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Ag}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right][\mathrm{Ag}]^{2}}{[\mathrm{Cu}]\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{(0.01) \times 1^{2}}{1 \times\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} Or [Ag + ] = 1.47 × 10 -9 M
solution: Cu is more reactive than silver, so that the cell is as Cu/Cu 2+ (0.01M) || Ag + (C)/Ag or cell reaction {tex}\mathrm{Cu}+2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Ag}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right][\mathrm{Ag}]^{2}}{[\mathrm{Cu}]\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{(0.01) \times 1^{2}}{1 \times\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} Or [Ag + ] = 1.47 × 10 -9 M
solution_html:
Cu is more reactive than silver, so that the cell is as Cu/Cu2+ (0.01M) || Ag+(C)/Ag or cell reaction
{tex}\mathrm{Cu}+2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Ag}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right][\mathrm{Ag}]^{2}}{[\mathrm{Cu}]\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{(0.01) \times 1^{2}}{1 \times\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}

Explain why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H2 at cathode and Cl2 at anode. Write overall reaction.
{tex}\left( \begin{gathered} E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V;\;E_{{H_2}O/{H_2}}^0 = - 0.83V \hfill \\ E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V;\;E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 = 1.23V \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right){/tex}
question_text: Explain why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H 2 at cathode and Cl 2 at anode. Write overall reaction. {tex}\left( \begin{gathered} E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V;\;E_{{H_2}O/{H_2}}^0 = - 0.83V \hfill \\ E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V;\;E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 = 1.23V \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right){/tex}
explanation: At cathode, the following reduction reactions can take place: {tex}Na_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to N{a_{(s)}};E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V{/tex} {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2}(g);\;E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^0 = OV{/tex} A reduction reaction with higher reduction potential is preferred. Therefore, the reaction at the cathode during electrolysis is {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex} This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H 2 at the cathode. At anode, the following oxidation reactions can take place: {tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex} {tex}2{H_2}{O_{(l)}} \to {O_{2(g)}} + 4H_{(aq)}^ + + 4{e^ - }E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 {/tex} = 1.23V At anode, the reaction with lower value of E 0 is preferred. But, due to overvoltage, oxidation of chloride ion occurs and chlorine gas is obtained. Hence, the reaction at the anode during electrolysis is {tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }\;E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex} This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives Cl 2 at the anode. The overall cell reaction is given below. {tex}{H^ + } + C{l^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2} + \frac{1}{2}C{l_2}{/tex}
solution: At cathode, the following reduction reactions can take place: {tex}Na_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to N{a_{(s)}};E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V{/tex} {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2}(g);\;E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^0 = OV{/tex} A reduction reaction with higher reduction potential is preferred. Therefore, the reaction at the cathode during electrolysis is {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex} This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H 2 at the cathode. At anode, the following oxidation reactions can take place: {tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex} {tex}2{H_2}{O_{(l)}} \to {O_{2(g)}} + 4H_{(aq)}^ + + 4{e^ - }E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 {/tex} = 1.23V At anode, the reaction with lower value of E 0 is preferred. But, due to overvoltage, oxidation of chloride ion occurs and chlorine gas is obtained. Hence, the reaction at the anode during electrolysis is {tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }\;E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex} This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives Cl 2 at the anode. The overall cell reaction is given below. {tex}{H^ + } + C{l^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2} + \frac{1}{2}C{l_2}{/tex}
solution_html:
At cathode, the following reduction reactions can take place:
{tex}Na_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to N{a_{(s)}};E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V{/tex}
{tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2}(g);\;E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^0 = OV{/tex}
A reduction reaction with higher reduction potential is preferred. Therefore, the reaction at the cathode during electrolysis is
{tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex}
This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H2 at the cathode.
At anode, the following oxidation reactions can take place:
{tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex}
{tex}2{H_2}{O_{(l)}} \to {O_{2(g)}} + 4H_{(aq)}^ + + 4{e^ - }E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 {/tex} = 1.23V
At anode, the reaction with lower value of E0 is preferred. But, due to overvoltage, oxidation of chloride ion occurs and chlorine gas is obtained. Hence, the reaction at the anode during electrolysis is
{tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }\;E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex}
This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives Cl2 at the anode.
The overall cell reaction is given below.
{tex}{H^ + } + C{l^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2} + \frac{1}{2}C{l_2}{/tex}

[Atomic mass: Cu = 63.5 g mol-1, Zn = 65 g mol-1; 1F = 96500 C mol-1]
question_text: When a steady current of 2A was passed through two electrolytic cells A and B containing electrolytes ZnSO 4 and CuSO 4 connected in series, 2g of Cu were deposited at the cathode of cell B. How long did the current flow? What mass of Zn was deposited at cathode of cell A? [Atomic mass: Cu = 63.5 g mol -1 , Zn = 65 g mol -1 ; 1F = 96500 C mol -1 ]
explanation: Steady current = 2A copper deposited at cathode of cell B = 2g Cell B contains CuSO 4 and reaction may be represented as, {tex}CuS{O_4} \to C{u^{2 + }} + SO_4^{2 - }{/tex} The reaction happens at cathode as, Cu 2+ + 2e - {tex}\to{/tex} Cu So, 1 mol Cu deposited by 2F charge 63.5 g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 2g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} x x = {tex}\frac{2 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5}{/tex} = 6078.74 C we know formula Q = It 6078.74 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t t = {tex}\frac{6078.74}{2}{/tex} = 3039.37 Sec From faraday's second law of electrolysis, {tex}\frac{\omega t \text { of } C u}{\omega t \text { of } Z n}=\frac{E q \omega t C u}{E q \cdot \omega t Z n}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{x}=\frac{63.5 / 2}{65 / 2}{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{2}{0.9769}{/tex} = 2.047 g weight of Zn deposited = 2.047 g
solution: Steady current = 2A copper deposited at cathode of cell B = 2g Cell B contains CuSO 4 and reaction may be represented as, {tex}CuS{O_4} \to C{u^{2 + }} + SO_4^{2 - }{/tex} The reaction happens at cathode as, Cu 2+ + 2e - {tex}\to{/tex} Cu So, 1 mol Cu deposited by 2F charge 63.5 g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 2g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} x x = {tex}\frac{2 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5}{/tex} = 6078.74 C we know formula Q = It 6078.74 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t t = {tex}\frac{6078.74}{2}{/tex} = 3039.37 Sec From faraday's second law of electrolysis, {tex}\frac{\omega t \text { of } C u}{\omega t \text { of } Z n}=\frac{E q \omega t C u}{E q \cdot \omega t Z n}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{x}=\frac{63.5 / 2}{65 / 2}{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{2}{0.9769}{/tex} = 2.047 g weight of Zn deposited = 2.047 g
solution_html:
Steady current = 2A
copper deposited at cathode of cell B = 2g
Cell B contains CuSO4 and reaction may be represented as,
{tex}CuS{O_4} \to C{u^{2 + }} + SO_4^{2 - }{/tex} 
The reaction happens at cathode as,
Cu2+ + 2e- {tex}\to{/tex} Cu
So, 1 mol Cu deposited by 2F charge
63.5 g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500
2g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} x 
x = {tex}\frac{2 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5}{/tex} = 6078.74 C 
we know formula
Q = It
6078.74 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t 
t = {tex}\frac{6078.74}{2}{/tex} = 3039.37 Sec
From faraday's second law of electrolysis,
{tex}\frac{\omega t \text { of } C u}{\omega t \text { of } Z n}=\frac{E q \omega t C u}{E q \cdot \omega t Z n}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{2}{x}=\frac{63.5 / 2}{65 / 2}{/tex} 
x = {tex}\frac{2}{0.9769}{/tex} = 2.047 g 
weight of Zn deposited = 2.047 g

question_text: The electrical resistance of a column of 0.05 mol L -1 NaOH solution of diameter 1 cm and length 50 cm is 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Calculate its resistivity, conductivity and molar conductivity.
explanation: Given, concentration of NaOH = 0.05M diameter = 1 cm, length = 50 cm, resistance= 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Area of cross section, A = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} = {tex}3.14 \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} {/tex} = 0.785 cm 2 Resistivity, {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R A}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.55 \times 10^{3} \times 0.785}{50 }{/tex} = 87.135 {tex}\Omega{/tex} cm Conductivity, {tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{1}{87.135}{/tex} = {tex}0.01148=1.148 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} Molar conductivity of solution {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{\sigma} \times 1000}{M}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.148 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000}{0.05}{/tex} = 229.6 Scm 2 mol -1
solution: Given, concentration of NaOH = 0.05M diameter = 1 cm, length = 50 cm, resistance= 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Area of cross section, A = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} = {tex}3.14 \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} {/tex} = 0.785 cm 2 Resistivity, {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R A}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.55 \times 10^{3} \times 0.785}{50 }{/tex} = 87.135 {tex}\Omega{/tex} cm Conductivity, {tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{1}{87.135}{/tex} = {tex}0.01148=1.148 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} Molar conductivity of solution {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{\sigma} \times 1000}{M}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.148 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000}{0.05}{/tex} = 229.6 Scm 2 mol -1

Given,
concentration of NaOH = 0.05M 
diameter = 1 cm,
length = 50 cm,
resistance= 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

Resistivity, {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R A}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.55 \times 10^{3} \times 0.785}{50 }{/tex} = 87.135{tex}\Omega{/tex} cm

question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K. 2Cr(s) + 3Fe 2+ (0.1M) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ (0.01M) + 3Fe(s) Given, {tex}E_{c{r^{3 + }}/cr}^o{/tex} =-0.74V, {tex}E_{F{e^{2 + }}/Fe}^o{/tex} = -0.44 V
explanation: Since oxidation of Cr is taking place in the given reaction, the chromium electrode is anode and as Fe is reduced in the reaction, Fe electrode is the cathode. The half-cell reactions are as follows. At anode Cr {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cr 3+ + 3e - ] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 At cathode Fe 2+ + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe] {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Overall reaction 2Cr + 3Fe 2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 3Fe Eo = E cathode - E anode = -0.44 - (-0.74) = 0.3V E = E o - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{n}\log \frac{{{{[C{r^{3 + }}]}^2}}}{{{{[F{e^{2 + }}]}^3}}}{/tex} Here, n = number of electrons transferred, i.e. equal to 6. = 0.3 - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{6}\log \frac{{{{[0.01]}^2}}}{{{{[0.1]}^3}}}{/tex} = 0.309 {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.31
solution: Since oxidation of Cr is taking place in the given reaction, the chromium electrode is anode and as Fe is reduced in the reaction, Fe electrode is the cathode. The half-cell reactions are as follows. At anode Cr {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cr 3+ + 3e - ] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 At cathode Fe 2+ + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe] {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Overall reaction 2Cr + 3Fe 2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 3Fe Eo = E cathode - E anode = -0.44 - (-0.74) = 0.3V E = E o - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{n}\log \frac{{{{[C{r^{3 + }}]}^2}}}{{{{[F{e^{2 + }}]}^3}}}{/tex} Here, n = number of electrons transferred, i.e. equal to 6. = 0.3 - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{6}\log \frac{{{{[0.01]}^2}}}{{{{[0.1]}^3}}}{/tex} = 0.309 {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.31
solution_html:
Since oxidation of Cr is taking place in the given reaction, the chromium electrode is anode and as Fe is reduced in the reaction, Fe electrode is the cathode. The half-cell reactions are as follows.
At anode Cr {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cr3+ + 3e-] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 
At cathode Fe2+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe] {tex}\times{/tex} 3 
Overall reaction 
2Cr + 3Fe2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr3+ + 3Fe
Eo = Ecathode - Eanode = -0.44 - (-0.74) = 0.3V
E = Eo - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{n}\log \frac{{{{[C{r^{3 + }}]}^2}}}{{{{[F{e^{2 + }}]}^3}}}{/tex}
Here, n = number of electrons transferred, i.e. equal to 6.
= 0.3 - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{6}\log \frac{{{{[0.01]}^2}}}{{{{[0.1]}^3}}}{/tex}
= 0.309 {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.31

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Batteries and fuel cells are very useful forms of galvanic cell. Any battery or cell that we use as a source of electrical energy is basically a galvanic cell. However, for a battery to be of practical use it should be reasonably light, compact and its voltage should not vary appreciably during its use. There are mainly two types of batteries - primary batteries and secondary batteries.
In the primary batteries, the reaction occurs only once and after use over a period of time the battery becomes dead and cannot be reused again, whereas the secondary batteries are rechargeable.
Production of electricity by thermal plants is not a very efficient method and is a major source of pollution. To solve this problem, galvanic cells are designed in such a way that energy of combustion of fuels is directly converted into electrical energy, and these are known as fuel cells. One such fuel cell was used in the Apollo space programme.
Answer the following questions:

  1. How do primary batteries differ from secondary batteries? (1)
  2. The cell potential of Mercury cell is 1.35 V, and remains constant during its life. Give reason. (1)
  3. Write the reactions involved in the recharging of the lead storage battery. (2)

    OR

    Write two advantages of fuel cells over other galvanic cells. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Batteries and fuel cells are very useful forms of galvanic cell. Any battery or cell that we use as a source of electrical energy is basically a galvanic cell. However, for a battery to be of practical use it should be reasonably light, compact and its voltage should not vary appreciably during its use. There are mainly two types of batteries - primary batteries and secondary batteries. In the primary batteries, the reaction occurs only once and after use over a period of time the battery becomes dead and cannot be reused again, whereas the secondary batteries are rechargeable. Production of electricity by thermal plants is not a very efficient method and is a major source of pollution. To solve this problem, galvanic cells are designed in such a way that energy of combustion of fuels is directly converted into electrical energy, and these are known as fuel cells. One such fuel cell was used in the Apollo space programme. Answer the following questions: How do primary batteries differ from secondary batteries? (1) The cell potential of Mercury cell is 1.35 V, and remains constant during its life. Give reason. (1) Write the reactions involved in the recharging of the lead storage battery. (2) OR Write two advantages of fuel cells over other galvanic cells. (2)
explanation: Primary batteries are not rechargeable while secondary batteries are rechargeable. Overall reaction does not involve any ion in solution whose concentration can change during its lifetime. Cathode: PbSO 4 (s) + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Pb(s) + SO 4 2- (aq) Anode: PbSO 4 (s) + 2H 2 O (I) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} PbO 2 (s) + SO 4 2- (aq) + 4H + (aq) + 2e - OR (i) More efficiency (ii) Pollution free
solution: Primary batteries are not rechargeable while secondary batteries are rechargeable. Overall reaction does not involve any ion in solution whose concentration can change during its lifetime. Cathode: PbSO 4 (s) + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Pb(s) + SO 4 2- (aq) Anode: PbSO 4 (s) + 2H 2 O (I) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} PbO 2 (s) + SO 4 2- (aq) + 4H + (aq) + 2e - OR (i) More efficiency (ii) Pollution free
solution_html:
1. Primary batteries are not rechargeable while secondary batteries are rechargeable.
  2. Overall reaction does not involve any ion in solution whose concentration can change during its lifetime.
  3. Cathode: PbSO4(s) + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Pb(s) + SO42-(aq)
    Anode: PbSO4(s) + 2H2O (I) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} PbO2(s) + SO42-(aq) + 4H+(aq) + 2e-

    OR

    (i) More efficiency (ii) Pollution free

    2 Al (s) + 3 Ni2+ (0.1 M) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Al3+ (0.01 M) + 3 Ni (s)
    Calculate its emf if {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = 1.41 V.

question_text: Represent the cell in which the following reaction takes place: 2 Al (s) + 3 Ni 2+ (0.1 M) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Al 3+ (0.01 M) + 3 Ni (s) Calculate its emf if {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = 1.41 V. How does molar conductivity vary with increase in concentration for strong electrolyte and weak electrolyte? How can you obtain limiting molar conductivity {tex}\left( \Lambda _ { \mathbf { m } } ^ { 0 } \right){/tex} for weak electrolyte?
explanation: Al(s) | Al 3 +(0.01M) || Ni 2 + (0.1 M) | Ni(s) {tex}E _ { ( \mathrm { cell } ) } = E _ { \mathrm { cell } } ^ { \ominus } \frac { 0.059 } { 6 } \frac { \left[ \mathrm { Al } ^ { 3 + } \right] ^ { 2 } } { \left[ \mathrm { Ni } ^ { 2 + } \right] ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}E _ { ( \mathrm { cell } ) } = 1.41 \mathrm { V } - \frac { 0.059 } { 6 } \log \frac { [ 0.01 ] ^ { 2 } } { [ 0.1 ] ^ { 3 } }{/tex} E(cell) = 1.4198 V Or E cell = 1.42 V {tex}\boldsymbol { \Lambda } _ { \mathbf { m } }{/tex} decreases with increase in concentration for both strong and weak electrolyte. The limiting molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte cannot be determined reliably by extrapolation. Instead, it can be expressed as a sum of ionic contributions, which can be evaluated from the limiting molar conductivities of strong electrolytes containing the same ions.
solution: Al(s) | Al 3 +(0.01M) || Ni 2 + (0.1 M) | Ni(s) {tex}E _ { ( \mathrm { cell } ) } = E _ { \mathrm { cell } } ^ { \ominus } \frac { 0.059 } { 6 } \frac { \left[ \mathrm { Al } ^ { 3 + } \right] ^ { 2 } } { \left[ \mathrm { Ni } ^ { 2 + } \right] ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}E _ { ( \mathrm { cell } ) } = 1.41 \mathrm { V } - \frac { 0.059 } { 6 } \log \frac { [ 0.01 ] ^ { 2 } } { [ 0.1 ] ^ { 3 } }{/tex} E(cell) = 1.4198 V Or E cell = 1.42 V {tex}\boldsymbol { \Lambda } _ { \mathbf { m } }{/tex} decreases with increase in concentration for both strong and weak electrolyte. The limiting molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte cannot be determined reliably by extrapolation. Instead, it can be expressed as a sum of ionic contributions, which can be evaluated from the limiting molar conductivities of strong electrolytes containing the same ions.
solution_html:
1. Al(s) | Al3+(0.01M) || Ni2+ (0.1 M) | Ni(s)
    {tex}E _ { ( \mathrm { cell } ) } = E _ { \mathrm { cell } } ^ { \ominus } \frac { 0.059 } { 6 } \frac { \left[ \mathrm { Al } ^ { 3 + } \right] ^ { 2 } } { \left[ \mathrm { Ni } ^ { 2 + } \right] ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
    {tex}E _ { ( \mathrm { cell } ) } = 1.41 \mathrm { V } - \frac { 0.059 } { 6 } \log \frac { [ 0.01 ] ^ { 2 } } { [ 0.1 ] ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
    E(cell) = 1.4198 V
    Or Ecell = 1.42 V
  2. {tex}\boldsymbol { \Lambda } _ { \mathbf { m } }{/tex} decreases with increase in concentration for both strong and weak electrolyte.
    The limiting molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte cannot be determined reliably by extrapolation. Instead, it can be expressed as a sum of ionic contributions, which can be evaluated from the limiting molar conductivities of strong electrolytes containing the same ions.
